message.content:
Research configuration inheritance and import patterns in modern tooling.

Focus on:
1. ESLint's extends system:
   - extends: ["eslint:recommended", "./other-config"]
   - How plugins work
   - Override patterns

2. TypeScript's extends:
   - tsconfig.json extends
   - Path resolution

3. Babel's extends and presets

4. CSS @import and cascade

5. Docker's multi-stage inheritance

6. Terraform modules

7. Kubernetes Kustomize overlays

Which system has:
- Best import/extend syntax?
- Clearest override semantics?
- Most flexible inheritance?

How could these patterns apply to "agent rules" that cascade through directories?

Return specific syntax examples and recommendations.

content.text: Vou pesquisar os padrões de configuração e herança em ferramentas modernas para entender qual sistema oferece a melhor abordagem para "regras de agente" em cascata.

input.query: ESLint extends configuration inheritance flat config 2025
TypeScript tsconfig extends path resolution inheritance

content.content:
Web search results for query: "ESLint extends configuration inheritance flat config 2025"

Links: [{"title":"Evolving flat config with extends - ESLint - Pluggable JavaScript Linter","url":"https://eslint.org/blog/2025/03/flat-config-extends-define-config-global-ignores/"},{"title":"Configuration Files - ESLint - Pluggable JavaScript Linter","url":"https://eslint.org/docs/latest/use/configure/configuration-files"},{"title":"Change Request: Make it easier to inherit flat configs from the repo root · Issue #18385 · eslint/eslint","url":"https://github.com/eslint/eslint/issues/18385"},{"title":"Configuration Migration Guide - ESLint - Pluggable JavaScript Linter","url":"https://eslint.org/docs/latest/use/configure/migration-guide"},{"title":"Change Request: Flat config extends · Issue #19116 · eslint/eslint","url":"https://github.com/eslint/eslint/issues/19116"},{"title":"How to use new \"flat config\" approach in Eslint? · vercel/next.js · Discussion #49337","url":"https://github.com/vercel/next.js/discussions/49337"},{"title":"ESLint 9 Flat config tutorial - DEV Community","url":"https://dev.to/aolyang/eslint-9-flat-config-tutorial-2bm5"},{"title":"Flat config rollout plans - ESLint - Pluggable JavaScript Linter","url":"https://eslint.org/blog/2023/10/flat-config-rollout-plans/"},{"title":"Plugin Migration to Flat Config - ESLint - Pluggable JavaScript Linter","url":"https://eslint.org/docs/latest/extend/plugin-migration-flat-config"},{"title":"ESLint's new config system, Part 2: Introduction to flat config - ESLint - Pluggable JavaScript Linter","url":"https://eslint.org/blog/2022/08/new-config-system-part-2/"}]

Here are the search results for ESLint flat config `extends` configuration inheritance in 2025:

## Key Findings

### Reintroduction of `extends` in Flat Config

Your eslint.config.js files can now use extends to simplify your configuration.(no content)ESLint ultimately realized that the best way to solve the set of problems users had with flat config was to reintroduce extends.(no content)The defineConfig() function allows you to specify an extends array in any object, and that array can contain objects, arrays, or strings (for plugin configs that follow the recommended approach).

### How `extends` Works in Flat Config

A configuration object uses extends to inherit all the traits of another configuration object or array (including rules, plugins, and language options) and can then modify all the options.(no content)This flattening behavior is designed to eliminate some of the confusion around the use of the spread operator (...) with the new configuration system. With defineConfig(), you never need to use the spread operator (unless you really want to!).

### Why `extends` Was Reintroduced

Now that more folks are moving to ESLint v9 and flat config, a common complaint is that it's difficult to extend configs from plugins.(no content)While many enjoyed the freedom to mix and match configurations using JavaScript, it turned out that a lot of users also found extending other configurations frustrating. One pointed criticism is that they never knew how to extend another configuration because some were objects, some were arrays, and not all plugins exposed their flat configs the same way.

### Using `extends` with Plugin Configs

We can normalize the experience of extending configs by reintroducing the extends key for flat configs. In flat configs, extends will not trigger any file loading. Instead, it will allow string identifiers for configs contained in plugins as well as objects.

### Use Cases for `extends`

For reusing configurations - When you want to inherit and build upon existing configurations from plugins, shareable packages, or predefined configs. For plugin configuration - When applying recommended or specific configurations from ESLint plugins (e.g., example/recommended). For shareable configurations - When using npm packages that export configuration objects (e.g., eslint-config-example). For predefined configurations - When using ESLint's built-in configurations like js/recommended or js/all. For modular configuration - When you want to compose multiple configuration sources into a single configuration object.

### ESLint v10 Plans

ESLint v10.0.0-beta.0 was pushed on December 12, 2025, which is a major release upgrade.(no content)When ESLint v10.0.0 is released, the eslintrc configuration system will be completely removed.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

content.content:
Web search results for query: "TypeScript tsconfig extends path resolution inheritance"

Links: [{"title":"TypeScript: TSConfig Option: extends","url":"https://www.typescriptlang.org/tsconfig/extends.html"},{"title":"TypeScript: TSConfig Reference - Docs on every TSConfig option","url":"https://www.typescriptlang.org/tsconfig/"},{"title":"Resolve tsconfig.json `extends` path using node_modules resolution logic · Issue #18865 · microsoft/TypeScript","url":"https://github.com/microsoft/TypeScript/issues/18865"},{"title":"Add a option of type inheritance from extended tsconfig · Issue #57616 · microsoft/TypeScript","url":"https://github.com/microsoft/TypeScript/issues/57616"},{"title":"tsconfig.json extends field node-style resolution broken · Issue #43245 · microsoft/TypeScript","url":"https://github.com/microsoft/TypeScript/issues/43245"},{"title":"Deep Dive Into Extending tsconfig.json","url":"https://echobind.com/post/deep-dive-into-extending-tsconfig-json"},{"title":"Configuration Inheritance · Issue #9876 · microsoft/TypeScript","url":"https://github.com/microsoft/TypeScript/issues/9876"},{"title":"Module & Module Resolution in Configuration Inheritance · Issue #18834 · microsoft/TypeScript","url":"https://github.com/Microsoft/TypeScript/issues/18834"},{"title":"TypeScript: TSConfig Option: paths","url":"https://www.typescriptlang.org/tsconfig/paths.html"},{"title":"vite-tsconfig-paths - npm","url":"https://www.npmjs.com/package/vite-tsconfig-paths"}]

Here's what I found about TypeScript `tsconfig.json` extends and path resolution inheritance:

## The `extends` Option

The value of `extends` is a string which contains a path to another configuration file to inherit from. The path may use Node.js style resolution. The configuration from the base file are loaded first, then overridden by those in the inheriting config file.

## Path Resolution Behavior

The problem is the paths in a tsconfig.json are relative to the folder that that specific tsconfig.json file is in, and not the tsconfig.json doing the extending.(no content)So in this example, all the paths in packages/tsconfig.json are relative to the packages/ folder, even when packages/tsconfig.json is extended (i.e. extending doesn't change the folder that paths are relative to).(no content)That means we always have to define `baseUrl`, `rootDir`, `outDir`, `include`, and `exclude` in the child tsconfig.json (the one that extends the base tsconfig.json).

## Inheritance Rules

Top-level members other than compilerOptions are completely overridden by following definitions. compilerOptions at the top level is merged (though its individual keys will still be overridden as though they were top-level keys). Relative paths found in the configuration file will be resolved relative to the configuration file they originated in.(no content)It's worth noting that when you extend a tsconfig.json, the properties in compilerOptions are merged. And when both files define the same property, the child tsconfig.json wins. However, this is not the case with include and exclude. If you define them in the child tsconfig.json, that exact value will be used; it won't be merged with the value from the base tsconfig.json.

## The `paths` Option

A series of entries which re-map imports to lookup locations relative to the `baseUrl` if set, or to the tsconfig file itself otherwise.(no content)Currently, when a package's tsconfig.json extends the root tsconfig, any paths defined in the package's tsconfig overwrite those from the base.(no content)This behavior requires developers to replicate the base paths in every package's tsconfig, leading to maintenance overhead and potential for inconsistency.

## Key Takeaways

1. **Relative paths stay relative to their origin** - Paths in a base config remain relative to where that base config file is located
2. **compilerOptions are merged** - Individual keys override, but the object is merged
3. **Top-level arrays are replaced** - `include`, `exclude`, and `files` are completely overridden, not merged
4. **`paths` are overwritten** - If a child defines `paths`, they completely replace the parent's paths (no merging)
5. **Node.js style resolution works for `extends`** - You can extend from packages in node_modules


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

Here's what I found about TypeScript `tsconfig.json` extends and path resolution inheritance:

## The `extends` Option

The value of `extends` is a string which contains a path to another configuration file to inherit from. The path may use Node.js style resolution. The configuration from the base file are loaded first, then overridden by those in the inheriting config file.

## Path Resolution Behavior

The problem is the paths in a tsconfig.json are relative to the folder that that specific tsconfig.json file is in, and not the tsconfig.json doing the extending.(no content)So in this example, all the paths in packages/tsconfig.json are relative to the packages/ folder, even when packages/tsconfig.json is extended (i.e. extending doesn't change the folder that paths are relative to).(no content)That means we always have to define `baseUrl`, `rootDir`, `outDir`, `include`, and `exclude` in the child tsconfig.json (the one that extends the base tsconfig.json).

## Inheritance Rules

Top-level members other than compilerOptions are completely overridden by following definitions. compilerOptions at the top level is merged (though its individual keys will still be overridden as though they were top-level keys). Relative paths found in the configuration file will be resolved relative to the configuration file they originated in.(no content)It's worth noting that when you extend a tsconfig.json, the properties in compilerOptions are merged. And when both files define the same property, the child tsconfig.json wins. However, this is not the case with include and exclude. If you define them in the child tsconfig.json, that exact value will be used; it won't be merged with the value from the base tsconfig.json.

## The `paths` Option

A series of entries which re-map imports to lookup locations relative to the `baseUrl` if set, or to the tsconfig file itself otherwise.(no content)Currently, when a package's tsconfig.json extends the root tsconfig, any paths defined in the package's tsconfig overwrite those from the base.(no content)This behavior requires developers to replicate the base paths in every package's tsconfig, leading to maintenance overhead and potential for inconsistency.

## Key Takeaways

1. **Relative paths stay relative to their origin** - Paths in a base config remain relative to where that base config file is located
2. **compilerOptions are merged** - Individual keys override, but the object is merged
3. **Top-level arrays are replaced** - `include`, `exclude`, and `files` are completely overridden, not merged
4. **`paths` are overwritten** - If a child defines `paths`, they completely replace the parent's paths (no merging)
5. **Node.js style resolution works for `extends`** - You can extend from packages in node_modules

Web search results for query: "Kubernetes Kustomize overlay inheritance patterns"

Links: [{"title":"Overlays - KodeKloud Notes","url":"https://notes.kodekloud.com/docs/Certified-Kubernetes-Application-Developer-CKAD/2025-Updates-Kustomize-Basics/Overlays"},{"title":"Support for mixins · Issue #759 · kubernetes-sigs/kustomize","url":"https://github.com/kubernetes-sigs/kustomize/issues/759"},{"title":"Declarative Management of Kubernetes Objects Using Kustomize | Kubernetes","url":"https://kubernetes.io/docs/tasks/manage-kubernetes-objects/kustomization/"},{"title":"Kustomize - Argo CD - Declarative GitOps CD for Kubernetes","url":"https://argo-cd.readthedocs.io/en/stable/user-guide/kustomize/"},{"title":"Mastering Configuration Management with Kustomize","url":"https://www.linkedin.com/pulse/mastering-configuration-management-kustomize-christopher-adamson-ln6xc"},{"title":"Stack Labs - Blog | Kustomize - The right way to do templating in Kubernetes","url":"https://blog.stack-labs.com/code/kustomize-101/"},{"title":"kustomize/examples/multibases/README.md at master · kubernetes-sigs/kustomize","url":"https://github.com/kubernetes-sigs/kustomize/blob/master/examples/multibases/README.md"},{"title":"Kubernetes Kustomize Tutorial (Comprehensive Guide)","url":"https://devopscube.com/kustomize-tutorial/"},{"title":"GitHub - kubernetes-sigs/kustomize: Customization of kubernetes YAML configurations","url":"https://github.com/kubernetes-sigs/kustomize"},{"title":"A Comprehensive Guide to Kustomize: Simplifying Kubernetes Configuration Management | by Sonam Shenoy | Medium","url":"https://medium.com/@sonamjournals/a-comprehensive-guide-to-kustomize-simplifying-kubernetes-configuration-management-7a7ef54f3d0b"}]

Here are the search results for Kubernetes Kustomize overlay inheritance patterns:

## Kustomize Overlay Inheritance Patterns

Kustomize is a tool for customizing Kubernetes resource configurations through overlay and inheritance of configs.(no content)It allows you to define base configs and then add customizations and overlays on top of those base configs in a flexible way.

### Core Concepts: Base and Overlays

Kustomize has two key concepts, Base and Overlays.(no content)With Kustomize we can reuse the base files (common YAMLs) across all environments and overlay (patches) specifications for each of those environments. Overlaying is the process of creating a customized version of the manifest file (base manifest + overlay manifest = customized manifest file).(no content)A typical Kustomize project is organized into two main sections: Base Configuration contains the shared Kubernetes configurations. Overlay Directories contain environment-specific folders where you can adjust or extend the base configuration using patches or by adding new resources.

### Typical Directory Structure

The Base folder represents the config that is going to be identical across all the environments. We put all the Kubernetes manifests in the Base. It has a default value that we can overwrite. On the other side, the Overlays folder allows us to customize the behavior on a per-environment basis. We can create an Overlay for each one of the environments. We specify all the properties and parameters that we want to overwrite & change.

### How Overlays Work

An overlay is just another kustomization, referring to the base, and referring to patches to apply to that base. This arrangement makes it easy to manage your configuration with git. The base could have files from an upstream repository managed by someone else. The overlays could be in a repository you own.(no content)Overlays customize bases in a targeted way for specific environments and use cases. Things like updating container images, adding resources, patching variables. Overlays inherit and modify bases.

### Multi-base/Inheritance Patterns

Kustomize encourages defining multiple variants - e.g. dev, staging and prod, as overlays on a common base. It's possible to create an additional overlay to compose these variants together - just declare the overlays as the bases of a new kustomization.(no content)You can create overlay hierarchies like: Overlay base without any bases defines cluster skeleton with services and pods; Overlay base_debug which inherits base and enables debug tools; Overlay base_debug_aws which adds AWS configs and secrets for services; and so on. This looks like inheritance in OOP (with multiple inheritance when you have multiple bases).

### Customization Methods

Patches directly modify resources in bases through partial definitions like changing an image name or replica count - a simple way to tweak bases. Replacements allow wholesale override of specific resources in a base, providing more control than patches. New resources can be added into the final configs via overlays, such as new ConfigMaps or Deployments.(no content)Overlays are not limited to patching existing resources; they can also introduce new resources that are unique to an environment. For example, in a production overlay, you might add a Grafana deployment.

### Using Components for Reusable Patterns

Kustomize components can be particularly helpful to implement a default/override pattern. Outside of Argo CD, to utilize components, you must add the components field to the kustomization.yaml that the Application references.

### Best Practices

Keeping base resources, overlays, and patches in separate directories helps maintain clarity and separation between different configurations. While working with Kustomize, it's essential to adhere to general Kubernetes best practices and try to keep the common values like namespace and common metadata in the base file.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

Here are the search results for Kubernetes Kustomize overlay inheritance patterns:

## Kustomize Overlay Inheritance Patterns

Kustomize is a tool for customizing Kubernetes resource configurations through overlay and inheritance of configs.(no content)It allows you to define base configs and then add customizations and overlays on top of those base configs in a flexible way.

### Core Concepts: Base and Overlays

Kustomize has two key concepts, Base and Overlays.(no content)With Kustomize we can reuse the base files (common YAMLs) across all environments and overlay (patches) specifications for each of those environments. Overlaying is the process of creating a customized version of the manifest file (base manifest + overlay manifest = customized manifest file).(no content)A typical Kustomize project is organized into two main sections: Base Configuration contains the shared Kubernetes configurations. Overlay Directories contain environment-specific folders where you can adjust or extend the base configuration using patches or by adding new resources.

### Typical Directory Structure

The Base folder represents the config that is going to be identical across all the environments. We put all the Kubernetes manifests in the Base. It has a default value that we can overwrite. On the other side, the Overlays folder allows us to customize the behavior on a per-environment basis. We can create an Overlay for each one of the environments. We specify all the properties and parameters that we want to overwrite & change.

### How Overlays Work

An overlay is just another kustomization, referring to the base, and referring to patches to apply to that base. This arrangement makes it easy to manage your configuration with git. The base could have files from an upstream repository managed by someone else. The overlays could be in a repository you own.(no content)Overlays customize bases in a targeted way for specific environments and use cases. Things like updating container images, adding resources, patching variables. Overlays inherit and modify bases.

### Multi-base/Inheritance Patterns

Kustomize encourages defining multiple variants - e.g. dev, staging and prod, as overlays on a common base. It's possible to create an additional overlay to compose these variants together - just declare the overlays as the bases of a new kustomization.(no content)You can create overlay hierarchies like: Overlay base without any bases defines cluster skeleton with services and pods; Overlay base_debug which inherits base and enables debug tools; Overlay base_debug_aws which adds AWS configs and secrets for services; and so on. This looks like inheritance in OOP (with multiple inheritance when you have multiple bases).

### Customization Methods

Patches directly modify resources in bases through partial definitions like changing an image name or replica count - a simple way to tweak bases. Replacements allow wholesale override of specific resources in a base, providing more control than patches. New resources can be added into the final configs via overlays, such as new ConfigMaps or Deployments.(no content)Overlays are not limited to patching existing resources; they can also introduce new resources that are unique to an environment. For example, in a production overlay, you might add a Grafana deployment.

### Using Components for Reusable Patterns

Kustomize components can be particularly helpful to implement a default/override pattern. Outside of Argo CD, to utilize components, you must add the components field to the kustomization.yaml that the Application references.

### Best Practices

Keeping base resources, overlays, and patches in separate directories helps maintain clarity and separation between different configurations. While working with Kustomize, it's essential to adhere to general Kubernetes best practices and try to keep the common values like namespace and common metadata in the base file.
